questions.question: Explain why UDP is called a connectionless protocol.
keyPoints: UDP does not establish a dedicated end-to-end connection before data transfer
No handshake mechanism like SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK in TCP
Each datagram is independent and can be sent immediately
No session is maintained between sender and receiver

questions.question: List four differences between UDP and TCP.
keyPoints: TCP is connection-oriented; UDP is connectionless
TCP provides reliable delivery with acknowledgment; UDP is unreliable
TCP provides flow control; UDP does not
TCP provides congestion control; UDP does not
TCP has larger header (20 bytes); UDP has smaller header (8 bytes)
TCP provides sequencing; UDP does not

questions.question: Describe the fields in the UDP header with their sizes.
keyPoints: Source Port: 16 bits (2 bytes) - identifies sending application
Destination Port: 16 bits (2 bytes) - identifies receiving application
Length: 16 bits (2 bytes) - total length of UDP header and data
Checksum: 16 bits (2 bytes) - optional error detection in IPv4, mandatory in IPv6
Total header size: 8 bytes (64 bits)

questions.question: Why is UDP preferred for real-time applications like VoIP?
keyPoints: Minimal overhead results in lower latency
No retransmission delay - speed is more critical than reliability
Real-time data becomes obsolete quickly, so lost packets are better skipped
No connection establishment saves time
Flow control and congestion control would cause unacceptable delays

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Checksum field in UDP? Explain briefly.
keyPoints: Provides error detection for UDP header and data
Verifies data integrity during transmission
Catches corrupted packets which are then discarded
Optional in IPv4 but mandatory in IPv6
Covers pseudo-header in addition to UDP header and data

questions.question: Calculate the maximum UDP payload size given that the IP maximum packet size is 65,535 bytes.
keyPoints: Maximum IP packet size: 65,535 bytes
Minimum IP header size: 20 bytes
UDP header size: 8 bytes
Maximum UDP payload = 65,535 - 20 - 8 = 65,507 bytes
Alternatively, UDP datagram max = 65,535 bytes total, so payload = 65,535 - 8 = 65,527 bytes including IP header

questions.question: Mention three well-known applications that use UDP and their port numbers.
keyPoints: DNS - Port 53 (Domain Name System)
DHCP - Ports 67 (server) and 68 (client)
TFTP - Port 69 (Trivial File Transfer Protocol)
SNMP - Ports 161/162 (Simple Network Management Protocol)
RIP - Port 520 (Routing Information Protocol)
VoIP applications - Various ports like 5060 (SIP)
